title: Pakistani Seekh Kebab

ingredients.name: minced mutton or beef (medium fat, not lean)

subs.name: 500 g chicken mince
subs.notes: cook 1–2 minutes less; chicken dries out faster

ingredients.name: onion, very finely chopped and squeezed dry

subs.name: 0.5 tsp ajwain (carom seeds)
subs.notes: gives a different but pleasant herbal note

steps.text: Squeeze the chopped onion in a clean cloth or your hands to remove as much moisture as possible — wet onion is the number one reason seekh kebabs fall apart. Combine onion with all the spices, herbs, ginger, and garlic in a bowl.

Add the mince to the bowl and knead firmly with your hands for 3–4 minutes, working the mixture until it becomes sticky and cohesive. A well-kneaded mince will hold its shape on the skewer. Refrigerate for 30 minutes if time permits.

Wet your hands and divide the mince into 8 equal portions. Mould each portion firmly around a flat-sided metal skewer (or kebab stick), pressing firmly and extending it into a 15 cm sausage shape. The grip must be tight or it will slide off during cooking.

Heat a tawa or griddle on high flame until very hot and brush with oil. Place the skewered kebabs on the tawa and cook for 4–5 minutes on one side without moving, until a good crust forms. Turn carefully and cook the second side for 4 minutes.

Baste with a little oil and cook, turning once more, for a further 3–4 minutes until fully cooked through and slightly charred on the outside. To check doneness, press the thickest part — it should feel firm, not squishy.
Slide the kebabs off the skewers and serve immediately on a plate lined with raw onion rings and lemon wedges, with mint chutney or raita on the side.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
critical_checks: Use an appropriate food thermometer and safe doneness guidance for the specific cut of meat.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: keema → 500 g chicken mince (cook 1–2 minutes less; chicken dries out faster); cumin → 0.5 tsp ajwain (carom seeds) (gives a different but pleasant herbal note)
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Cook on a cast-iron tawa on the highest flame your hob allows to replicate the tandoor's searing heat. No metal skewers? Shape the mixture into oval flat patties (an inch thick) and pan-fry — they'll taste identical. For an air-fryer version, cook at 200°C for 10–12 minutes, turning halfway. The flat seekh shape is easier to hold on any Indian tawa than a round kebab.
